primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (acute decompensated heart failure)

pathophysiology_summary: In a patient with known chronic heart failure, a missed diuretic dose and an untreated night of sleep apnoea (worsening nocturnal hypoxaemia and afterload) together precipitate acute decompensation: elevated left ventricular filling pressures back up into the pulmonary circulation, causing fluid to transude into the alveoli. This produces paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea with profound orthopnoea (worse supine, better upright, as gravity redistributes fluid away from the upper lung fields), pink frothy sputum (alveolar fluid mixed with surfactant and a small amount of blood from ruptured capillaries), and severe hypoxaemia. The severely elevated blood pressure reflects both the underlying hypertensive substrate and acute sympathetic activation, further increasing afterload and worsening pump failure in a self-perpetuating cycle. This is a hypertensive acute heart failure crisis with a high risk of progression to hypoxaemic respiratory arrest without urgent afterload/preload reduction and ventilatory support. Via telehealth, auscultation, JVP, and BNP cannot be assessed, but the reported orthopnoea, frothy sputum, and profound exhaustion are sufficient to mandate immediate ambulance dispatch with CPAP/BiPAP capability.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden orthopnoea with a sensation of drowning and known heart failure history.
Causes acute severe dyspnoea, though pink frothy sputum and profound orthopnoea are atypical for a primary airway process.
Must be screened for in any acute severe dyspnoea, though frothy sputum and orthopnoea in a known heart failure patient favour a cardiogenic cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Known heart failure with sudden orthopnoea and frothy sputum should place cardiogenic pulmonary oedema as leading from the opening complaint.

differential.diagnosis: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, precipitated by missed diuretic and untreated OSA
differential.evidence_basis: Profound orthopnoea (DI-001), pink frothy sputum (DI-002), missed furosemide dose and unused CPAP (DI-003), and extreme exhaustion (DI-004) together confirm decompensated heart failure with identifiable precipitants.
Pink frothy sputum and profound orthopnoea (rather than expiratory wheeze pattern) are inconsistent with a primary airway process.
Frothy sputum and clear orthopnoea pattern in a known heart failure patient with identifiable precipitants (missed diuretic, untreated OSA) favour a cardiogenic cause over PE.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage cardiogenic pulmonary oedema should be the sole leading diagnosis and the AI Doctor should already be directing immediate emergency ambulance dispatch.

differential.diagnosis: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (acute decompensated heart failure)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires examination (crackles, S3, JVP), BNP, and chest X-ray, unavailable remotely. Emergency escalation with CPAP/BiPAP capability is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A management plan without immediate 000 dispatch and correct upright positioning at this stage constitutes a critical failure.

red_flags.description: Profound orthopnoea — unable to breathe at all except sitting bolt upright
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic and severe marker of pulmonary venous congestion from left ventricular failure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Patient advised to lie down and rest, worsening pulmonary congestion and hypoxaemia.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic sign of alveolar flooding in acute pulmonary oedema.

red_flags.description: Extreme exhaustion from the work of breathing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Respiratory muscle fatigue in this context is an ominous sign preceding respiratory arrest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Impending respiratory failure not recognised; patient arrests before help arrives.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe hypoxaemia supporting the need for immediate oxygen and ventilatory support.

red_flags.description: Bilateral fine crackles, S3 gallop, elevated JVP, and severe hypertension on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms left ventricular failure with fluid overload; requires auscultation and BP measurement unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — escalation must rely on the reported orthopnoea, frothy sputum, and exhaustion above.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Advising the patient to lie flat to rest
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A well-meaning but dangerous instinct to have the patient lie down and rest would worsen pulmonary venous congestion; the correct advice is to remain sitting bolt upright with legs dependent.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-weighting respiratory exhaustion as a marker of imminent arrest
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor might focus only on the frothy sputum/orthopnoea and fail to recognise that reported extreme exhaustion from breathing effort is itself a marker of impending respiratory failure requiring the most urgent possible response.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation for crackles and S3 gallop
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000), Priority 1 dispatch with CPAP/BiPAP capability for pre-hospital non-invasive ventilatory support
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Advising the patient to lie down, wait, or take an extra diuretic dose at home without emergency escalation risks rapid progression to hypoxaemic respiratory arrest.
